params.text:
Making a best-interests decision

If — and only if — a person is assessed as lacking capacity for a decision, any action taken on their behalf must be in their best interests. The MCA deliberately does not define ‘best interests’ narrowly; instead it gives a checklist of things the decision-maker must consider.

  • Do not make the decision merely on the basis of the person’s age, appearance, condition or behaviour.
  • Consider whether the person is likely to regain capacity, and if so whether the decision can wait.
  • Encourage and support the person to take part in the decision as far as they are able.
  • Consider the person’s past and present wishes and feelings, especially any written statement made when they had capacity.
  • Consider their beliefs and values and any other factors they would likely have taken into account.
  • Take into account the views of anyone named by the person, carers, family, an attorney or a deputy — where it is practical and appropriate to consult them.

Best interests is about far more than physical health or safety. Emotional wellbeing, dignity, relationships and the person’s own values all count.

Who must you consult?

A best-interests decision should never be made in isolation. Wherever it is practical and appropriate, the decision-maker must take account of the views of:

  • Anyone the person has previously named as someone to be consulted.
  • People engaged in caring for the person or interested in their welfare — family, friends and care staff.
  • Any attorney appointed under a Lasting Power of Attorney, or a deputy appointed by the Court of Protection.

Consulting is not the same as handing over the decision. Family members do not have an automatic right to decide — unless they hold the relevant legal authority. Their role is to help build a picture of what the person would have wanted.

params.question: Once a person is found to lack capacity, staff can choose whatever option is safest and most convenient for the service, without needing to consider what the person themselves would have wanted.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Best interests is not about convenience. The person’s own wishes, feelings, beliefs and values must be central to the decision.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. A best-interests decision must weigh the person’s past and present wishes, feelings, beliefs and values — not simply what is safest or most convenient for the service.
